comments.post_body:
Hiring for experience feels safe.
Hiring for potential feels bold.

But only the second one drives long-term success.

Love this post from Rob Dance

Some of the best people I’ve worked with started in roles they weren’t “ready” for.

They didn’t check every box.
But they showed up ready to learn fast.
And that’s what separated them from the rest.

When you hire people who are hungry to grow:

+ You get new ideas, not recycled methods.
+ You get fresh energy, not burnout.
+ You get loyalty, not entitlement.

Because passionate people treat your mission like it’s their own.

As leaders, our job isn’t to find the most “qualified” person.
It’s to create an environment:
→ Where people become more qualified every single month.

That’s what real leadership does:
→ It develops potential into performance.

So next time you’re reviewing applications,
look beyond the bullet points.

Ask yourself:
→ Do they light up when they talk about learning?
→ Do they take responsibility for growth?
→ Do they make me want to work harder just by being around them?

That’s the hire you make.

P.S. Who’s one person you’ve seen grow faster than their experience should’ve allowed? Tag them 👇
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the importance of hiring for potential rather than experience, as it drives long-term success and brings fresh ideas and energy to the team. The commenter is responding to the idea that leaders should focus on developing potential into performance by creating an environment that fosters growth and learning.

comments.post_body:
For the love of everything that is holy in the world, please PLEASE take time to personalize your GPT model so it can sound like you, and my feed can look like actual people wrote it— and make me want to read your content!

It’s not you, it’s your GPT!

To make content yours, avoid/delete/rewrite (if at all possible):

• Contrast framing (the classic “not X, but Y” such as “This isn’t a feature, it’s a movement”
• Purpose compression (short sentences that reduce complexity into intent, such as “The signal is clear”)
• Minimalism that feels engineered (Statements such as “designed to listen”, or “trust, operationalized”)

Above all, if you were never a writer before and are now posting best-selling, philosophical content that is not aligned with your brand, please stop!

I really do want to engage with your content. As long as it sounds like it’s YOURS!

Brand it! Please brand it. It takes one minute!

Miri

#AIWorldProblems #Stop
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the overuse of generic GPT model content, urging users to personalize their models to make their content sound more authentic and engaging. The commenter is pleading with users to brand their content and make it their own, rather than relying on clichés and overly engineered language.

comments.comment_body: I can't support Miri enough on this! If it took you 5 minutes to generate a 65 page document, YOU didn't write it. AND... You probably didn't read it either. So please don't ask me to read it.

comments.post_body:
Most people told me I was too late to the game.
I left corporate at 47.
Got married at 45.

We're taught early that potential has a shelf life.

If you don't hit certain milestones by a certain age, 
you missed your chance.

I don’t believe that anymore.

I know too many people who built something real after 40:
→ After a layoff
→ After divorce
→ After illness
→ After years of playing it safe

My friend beat cancer at 42. Then went to medical school.
She's 67 now. Still practicing. One of the best in women's health.

Bernie Marcus got fired at 46.
He could've seen it as the end.

Instead, he started Home Depot at 49.
Today it's worth $343 billion.

Here's what I've learned:

The first 40 years weren't wasted. They built me.

The grief, the mistakes, the repairs, the hard lessons I didn't want to learn.

All of it matters now at 56.

There's no deadline on becoming who you're meant to be.

If you're rebuilding, restarting, or finally doing the thing you've been putting off, you're not behind.

You're right on time.

📌 Save this for the days doubt gets loud.
♻️ Share it with someone who thinks they're behind.
🔔 Follow Melina Panetta for more on building after 40
___
Join 600+ Leaders crossing the bridge here: https://lnkd.in/gWYD3-kx
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about overcoming the notion that one's potential has a shelf life and that it's never too late to start anew, with the author sharing personal anecdotes and examples of successful people who achieved their goals after the age of 40. The post aims to inspire and motivate those who feel they are behind in life, encouraging them to keep moving forward and pursue their passions.

comments.post_body:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how Mahjong is becoming a popular networking platform for women in tech, replacing traditional methods with a more personal and human approach, and the commenter is responding to this idea of Mahjong as the 'IRL LinkedIn for women'. The post highlights the growth of Mahjong events and their benefits, such as fostering real conversations and collaborations, and the commenter is likely sharing their thoughts on this emerging trend.

comments.comment_body: Love this Miri. Congrats!
comments.post_body:
I’m deeply honored to be featured by CIO TIMES as one of 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟱 𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗜𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗰 𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗧𝗼 𝗪𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱. I dedicate this recognition to every woman entrepreneur daring to make a difference in the world!

When Stacey and I co-founded Empressa AI, we took a leap of faith to solve a problem we couldn’t ignore. 

Today, our innovation speaks for itself. We built 𝗮 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁-𝗼𝗳-𝗶𝘁𝘀-𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝗜 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝗯𝘆 𝘄𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻, 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘄𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻, 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗴𝗮𝗽.

Empressa wears two crowns: 

👑  We’re enabling women experts to build their digital legacy and earn royalties every time their insights guide others. This means that a mid-career strategist in Ohio or a grassroots founder in Nairobi can transform her hard-earned experience into a source of income and impact. 
👑 Simultaneously, rising women leaders gain 24/7 access to tailored mentorship, bridging the gap between potential and opportunity.

At the heart of this journey are a few core beliefs that guide me every day:

	•	𝗘𝗺𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗵𝘆 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘀𝘁: “At Empressa, we don’t just build networks—we build trust, empathy, and lifelong connections.”  Real innovation starts with listening and understanding.
	•	𝗜𝗻𝗻𝗼𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻: We can’t wait 134 years for gender parity in the workforce – I believe #AI can be an equalizer to accelerate equity. When women build the tech, they build a future for everyone.
	•	𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽: True leadership requires the courage to show up authentically, even when it’s hard. I’ve learned that owning my whole story — the failures, lessons, and triumphs — is actually one of my greatest strengths.

I’m extremely grateful and proud of the team behind Empressa taking this mission to life (Ronnie Hamilton, Siddhartha C., Akshat Rana,PMP®, Laura Jimenez and Viktoriya Lindner), our Founding Members (tagged below) who are the lifeblood of our community, and the continued support from our Empressalies!

If you’re a woman expert or a woman rising leader or a supporter of women, join us at Empressa.ai!

Full article here: https://lnkd.in/e7we2K-P

#AIForWomen #WomenLeaders #WomenInTech #CIOTimes #Empressa #TrustworthyAI #WomenEmpoweringWomen #Leadership #Founder
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about being featured as one of the most iconic business leaders to watch in 2025, and is dedicating the recognition to women entrepreneurs making a difference, while also highlighting their own company Empressa AI's mission to close the mentorship and opportunity gap for women. The post discusses Empressa's innovative AI platform and its impact on women's empowerment and leadership.

comments.post_body:
🎮✨ Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! ✨🎮

This summer was an incredible journey of learning and growth. I joined the Experiences and Platforms for Developers: Creator Onboarding, Resources, and Support (XPD-CORS) team, contributing to a greenfield project aimed at transforming how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games.

As a TPM, I led partner studio discussions, authored a feature spec, and collaborated across time zones with a designer to produce Figma designs.

As a SWE, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it to XPD multiple times.

Other highlights included placing 3rd in the Intern Celebration Hackathon, earning 2nd in Halo Reach: Firefight during the Xbox Intern Gaming Tournament, winning a tour of Mojang Studios!

I also played a ton of Sand Volleyball and started reading One Piece🏴‍☠️

I’m deeply grateful to those who made this internship unforgettable:
💚 My pod, the ThunderDome — Sunday Ochigbo and Raul Ballines (FIFA Fridays forever, trust).
💚 My manager Suvir Arora and (formerly) Alex Blount 
💚 My mentors Brian Trevethan  and Greg Gonyea 
💚 The team — Anthony Keller, Maria Lopez Quesada, Daniel Jones, David Kushmerick, Kyle Sweeney, Jason Williams, Eddie Iglesias III, George Ju, and Ai Fukuda 
💚 Jerry Zhu for being an invaluable mentor to the ThunderDome and the best step-podmate
💚 My interviewers and recruiter Luis Naranjo, Karla Larriva, and Jessica Nguyen  for helping make this experience possible.
💚 My hackathon team Greenlens: Jada Sheppard, Geo Johnson, Sloan Howard, Yemi Afolayanka, Temiloluwa Okelowo, and Tryphena Bossman 

A huge thank you as well to all the Xbox interns and to Intern Program lead Teresa Wong for the incredible events and building such an amazing community. 

All the challenges, guidance, encouragement, and camaraderie made my first internship a truly unforgettable one. 

#Microsoft #Xbox #Internship #GameDevelopment #Tech #MicrosoftExplore #MicrosoftIntern #XboxIntern
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the author's final day of their Explore internship at Xbox, where they shared their experiences and accomplishments as a SWE and TPM, and expressed gratitude to their team and mentors. The commenter is likely congratulating the author on the completion of their internship and their achievements.

comments.post_body:
I cannot begin to express my gratitude to Georgia Tech. Over the past four years I have learned, experienced, and grown so much, both intellectually and personally. I am stepping into the future forever changed by everything the institution has been able to offer me over me. 

But beyond the projects and presentations, it’s the people who made this journey unforgettable. My professors, mentors, and peers have been a constant source of inspiration and support. I truly believe it is their encouragement that helped create the drive and passion I now see within my own work. 

As I welcome in this next chapter, I carry with me not just a degree, but a mindset shaped by Georgia Tech’s commitment to excellence and innovation. The road ahead may be uncertain, but I am confident that the skills and experiences gained here will guide me toward making meaningful contributions in the world of design.

To my fellow graduates, congratulations! To my professors, thank you!

Today I am incredibly proud to share that I have graduated from the Georgia Institute of Technology with a B.S. in Industrial Design with highest honors!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is expressing gratitude and pride after graduating from Georgia Tech with a degree in Industrial Design, thanking the institution and its people for their support and inspiration. They are now looking forward to the future, confident that their experiences and skills will guide them in making meaningful contributions in the world of design.

comments.comment_body: Sapjah Zapotitla, you all did a great job hosting the TEDx event! You were especially helpful in making sure everyone had what they needed and felt supported. Congratulations on your success, it was a real pleasure getting to meet you.
comments.post_body:
Reflecting on an incredible journey! ✨

I'm grateful to Michael T. Dominik, Ph.D. for bringing me onboard as a University Innovation Fellows, even as a Psychology major! Rowan University College of Science & Mathematics
I met the most outstanding students from Rowan University: Elizabeth Pennington, Victoria Van, and Aiden Tahmazian. 

Since August 2024, we embarked on a mission to make Rowan "better." So if you need students to get it done, you can count on these three. They've inspired me to step out of my box and achieve what might seem unachievable (in the moment). 


Highlights of our journey include:
- Attending the UIF meetup at University of Twente in the Netherlands, where we networked with students and alumni worldwide and joined workshops in the DesignLab University of Twente. **Included 2 pictures**
- Of course, hosting a successful TEDx event last Friday, featuring inspiring speakers who shared their perspectives on innovation and entrepreneurship: Shawn Hill, Riccardo Dale, Siena Rampulla, Jerah Siegal, Fred Hodge Jr, Daniel Jones, and Noah Kouhoupt.

My favorite part? 
- Conducting interviews with potential TEDx speakers 
- Capturing the event through my lens 📸

Overall, witnessing this event has brought me one step closer to my own TED Talk dream! And although the event has concluded, I encourage my team, our supporters, and the speakers to continue doing great things. The momentum we've built together will propel us forward! 


At the end, check out some of my favorite captures from the event. 🫶🏽
#UniversityInnovationFellows #TEDxRowanUniversity #Innovation #Entrepreneurship #JustTheBeginning
comments.context_summary: The commenter is reflecting on their incredible journey as a University Innovation Fellow, highlighting their experiences and achievements, including attending a UIF meetup and hosting a successful TEDx event, and expressing gratitude to their team and supporters. They are celebrating the momentum built and looking forward to continuing to achieve great things, including their own TED Talk dream.

comments.comment_body: I love this take on the topic Claire, and I completely agree that hiring new talent is always a commitment. Especially when young in career!
comments.post_body:
My hot take - 

This article highlights the importance of hiring with a growth mindset. To take on a green member of your workforce is to also commit to molding them and helping them grow into a valuable team player. Hiring someone new is a two way commitment. Sad to see so many companies not taking the time to mentor and grow alongside Gen Z. Makes me super thankful for the mentorship I do have.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to an article about hiring with a growth mindset, emphasizing the importance of mentoring and growing with new employees, particularly Gen Z. They express gratitude for their own mentorship while criticizing companies that neglect to invest time in developing their younger workforce members.

Happy #InternationalPodcastDay! 🎙️ It’s been an incredible experience hosting an award-winning podcast, Beyond the Brown & Gold, with my closest friends and fellow Rowan Alumni. Being back in the WGLS-FM studios, where it all began, has been a full-circle moment—bringing back great memories while giving us a creative outlet to share new stories. Huge thanks to all of our guests and to everyone who’s tuned in and supported us along the way. Here’s to more conversations, laughs and nostalgia!

🎧 go.rowan.edu/listen

comments.comment_body: Congrats Kevin!
comments.post_body:
For those closer to me, the second half of July and the month of August have been absolutely brutal months. After dealing with an unexpected family tragedy, and then having position eliminated, it has been one of the more trying periods of my life. But with the support of my loving wife, my amazing children, and the brilliant colleagues I've had the privilege of working with. I am entering September hopeful. I am happy to announce that I have accepted a position as an Apps and Innovation Sales Director with MS Federal. I am excited to start this new chapter in my life working with this team of truly amazing people.  

I want to thank the following people for their support during this time.  Brooke Hamilton, Mike Smucny, Andy Olenik, Andy Hickl, Steve Coleman, Mandar Inamdar, Richard Zak, Jamie Snedaker, Sean Graine, Andrew Hassett, Breanna Stryker, Bashir Partovi, Brad Armstrong, Clarice Mack, Chad Peters, John McCabe, Kimberly Nelson, George Taylor Corbett, Mary Wahl, David Weinstein, Ryan Campbell, Alex Kreilein, Brandon R, Juan Carlos López, Craig Pomeroy, Ben Hilburn, Mohit Garg

comments.comment_body: Jason Guarracino yeah right brother. Tell that to my back!!
comments.post_body:
Fresh off a flight from Key West, Florida, Daniel Jones '05 joins the program to talk about his rocky road from Rowan University to working for Xbox. Besides his work in the world of artificial intelligence, Jones has a love for jiu jitsu and mentoring young engineers. Jones shared wisdom regarding technology, being a parent, and much more during a fun trip down memory lane.

🎧 Hear more of Daniel's story at go.rowan.edu/listen.

I’m happy to share that I’ve obtained a new leadership certification: XLDR229 - Leading Innovation from Stanford Online! 🎓

Thanks Bob Sutton for compiling the course syllabus - bringing in real-world case studies, organizational research, and creating clarity through all the jargon monoxide that exists in typical leadership training! 🥳

Thanks Robyn Woodman for making me aware of this program and inviting me to dive deeper into organizational psychology! 🙌

Be the team energizer, sell your ideas with enthusiasm, and have a collective pride in what you build! 🎉
